jokes.text: Yeah. Well, they say If you're going to quit, You better do it Early in the day.
jokes.setup: Phil arrives early to work
jokes.punchline: Reveals he's there early specifically to quit, treating it like mundane advice
jokes.explanation: Perfectly dry delivery of absurd logic - treating quitting like a chore with optimal timing. Classic Larry Sanders deadpan structure.

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: Yeah. Well, they say If you're going to quit, You better do it Early in the day.

jokes.text: It's about a bunch of guys In a ska band in seattle.
jokes.setup: Phil describing his innovative single-camera sitcom
jokes.punchline: The 'revolutionary' concept is incredibly specific and niche
jokes.explanation: The specificity and randomness of 'ska band in Seattle' reveals Phil's limited creative vision despite his grandiose setup.

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: It's about a bunch of guys In a ska band in seattle.

jokes.text: Very dark. Dark like friends?
jokes.setup: Phil describing his show as 'very dark'
jokes.punchline: Comparison to Friends, the least dark show imaginable
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy and ironic contrast. The juxtaposition of 'very dark' with Friends is brilliantly absurd and quotable.

dialogue.line: Very dark. Dark like friends?

jokes.text: You hear that sound, phil? I believe that's The needle breaking On the bullshit-o-meter.
jokes.setup: Phil giving elaborate health excuses for quitting
jokes.punchline: Artie's colorful metaphor for detecting lies
jokes.explanation: Classic Artie - inventive profanity substitute with perfect character voice. The 'bullshit-o-meter' is a memorable Artie-ism.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: You hear that sound, phil? I believe that's The needle breaking On the bullshit-o-meter.

jokes.text: Did you say 'frigging'? I'm trying to cut down On my cuss words. Mother call? Yeah. Yeah. She says hello.
jokes.setup: Artie using 'frigging' instead of stronger profanity
jokes.punchline: Reveals his mother called, explaining the temporary clean language
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Artie's tough guy persona cracking under maternal influence. Relatable and specific.

dialogue.line: Did you say 'frigging'?

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: I'm trying to cut down On my cuss words.
Mother call?

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Yeah. Yeah. She says hello.

jokes.text: Of 'my shoe in your rectumitis.'
jokes.setup: Phil claiming serious health problems
jokes.punchline: Artie's fake medical condition threat
jokes.explanation: Artie's creative threat using medical terminology. The '-itis' suffix adds sophistication to a crude threat.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Of 'my shoe in your rectumitis.'

jokes.text: Like cher's ass.
jokes.setup: Describing handcrafted artisanal pens
jokes.punchline: Comparison to Cher's famously artificial posterior
jokes.explanation: Classic Hank non-sequitur. The comparison is random but oddly apt - both are artificially enhanced 'works of art.'

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Like cher's ass.

jokes.text: It's all tears, cartwheels, And a hard-on.
jokes.setup: Larry wanting to see more enthusiasm about the pen gift
jokes.punchline: Hank's bizarre description of his internal emotional state
jokes.explanation: Peak Hank absurdity - mixing innocent emotions with inappropriate physical reactions. The specificity and randomness make it memorably weird.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: It's all tears, cartwheels, And a hard-on.

jokes.text: How would you feel If one of the characters Was black?
jokes.setup: Network praising Phil's perfect pilot
jokes.punchline: Immediate suggestion to change fundamental aspect
jokes.explanation: Brutal satire of network interference. The timing after effusive praise makes the undermining more painful and realistic.

dialogue.speaker: Network Executive
dialogue.line: How would you feel If one of the characters Was black?

jokes.text: Well, we got in our head Now that he's black, So we started calling him Keshaun.
jokes.setup: Phil's character was named Ted
jokes.punchline: Network already renamed him with stereotypical name
jokes.explanation: Sharp industry satire showing casual racism and presumptuous control. The matter-of-fact delivery makes it more uncomfortable.

dialogue.speaker: Network Executive
dialogue.line: Well, we got in our head Now that he's black, So we started calling him Keshaun.

jokes.text: I said, 'thank you. I've been sitting All day.'
jokes.setup: Lap dance story - stripper points to chair
jokes.punchline: Misunderstands gesture as politeness about seating
jokes.explanation: Perfect misdirection joke with complete character logic. Katz's neurotic misreading of sexual situation is believable and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Jonathan Katz
dialogue.line: I said, 'thank you. I've been sitting All day.'

jokes.text: I have been on stand-by For my own phantas For 5 1/2 years now.
jokes.setup: Hank congratulating on the pen gift
jokes.punchline: Reveals his own desperate waiting for similar recognition
jokes.explanation: Pure Hank pathology - his desperate need for Larry's approval manifested in pen envy. The specific timeframe adds painful detail.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: I have been on stand-by For my own phantas For 5 1/2 years now.

jokes.text: Who the fuck am i, hootie?
jokes.setup: Being the one black guy in a white band
jokes.punchline: Reference to Hootie & the Blowfish's Black lead singer
jokes.explanation: Brilliant pop culture reference that perfectly captures the situation. Economical, specific, and cuts to the heart of the tokenism issue.

dialogue.speaker: Dave Chappelle
dialogue.line: Who the fuck am i, hootie?

jokes.text: Maybe that's why They're sleepless.
jokes.setup: Discussing lack of Black people in Seattle
jokes.punchline: Connects to 'Sleepless in Seattle' movie title
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay connecting the movie title to racial demographics. Quick wit that shows Dave thinking on his feet.

dialogue.speaker: Dave Chappelle
dialogue.line: Maybe that's why They're sleepless.

jokes.text: Every week A patron is murdered! I got to solve it. Kind of like a hip-hop Crime-solving type thing.
jokes.setup: Dave proposing alternative DJ concept
jokes.punchline: Completely abandons the original concept for murder mystery
jokes.explanation: Shows how completely the original concept has been abandoned. The casual 'every week a patron is murdered' is absurdly matter-of-fact.

dialogue.speaker: Dave Chappelle
dialogue.line: Every week A patron is murdered! I got to solve it. Kind of like a hip-hop Crime-solving type thing.

jokes.text: Oh, man, this is a bic. I'm talking about A rare black pen.
jokes.setup: Looking for the expensive lost pen
jokes.punchline: Artie's dismissal of ordinary pen when crisis demands luxury
jokes.explanation: Shows Artie's standards and the absurd importance placed on the pen. Visual contrast between cheap and expensive writing instruments.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Oh, man, this is a bic. I'm talking about A rare black pen.

jokes.text: It's not that I'm worried That, uh... Larry likes you More than he likes me, Because, you know-- fuck it-- You know, we both know he does.
jokes.setup: Hank trying to seem casual about Larry's preferences
jokes.punchline: Immediately admits the painful truth he was denying
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank moment - trying to play it cool but immediately cracking. The self-aware admission of his lower status is painfully honest.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: It's not that I'm worried That, uh... Larry likes you More than he likes me, Because, you know-- fuck it-- You know, we both know he does.

jokes.text: He's such a cheap fucker.
jokes.setup: Hearing Larry gave a cheap travel clock
jokes.punchline: Hank's bitter reaction to Larry's typical cheapness
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective character moment. Hank's immediate bitterness reveals his ongoing resentment about Larry's treatment.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: He's such a cheap fucker.

jokes.text: He went down, He got in his own car. He drove there. He parked it. He got out. He walked in the doors, And he pulled out His own personal Credit card?
jokes.setup: Learning Larry bought a gift himself
jokes.punchline: Breaking down the impossible concept of Larry making personal effort
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation showing how unprecedented Larry's gesture is. Each step becomes more unbelievable, building to the credit card revelation.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: He went down, He got in his own car. He drove there. He parked it. He got out. He walked in the doors, And he pulled out His own personal Credit card?

jokes.text: [Artie crying after learning about the lost pen]
jokes.setup: The tough guy Artie learning about Larry's rare generous gesture being lost
jokes.punchline: Artie breaks down emotionally over the lost pen
jokes.explanation: Powerful character moment - the hardened Artie moved to tears by Larry's kindness being wasted. Visual comedy from seeing him vulnerable.

jokes.text: First I'm hootie, Now I'm fucking kunte kinte, The deejay?
jokes.setup: Dave's frustration with stereotypical character suggestions
jokes.punchline: References both Hootie and Roots character as examples of tokenism
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation that calls back to earlier Hootie joke while adding Roots reference. Sharp critique of how white writers approach Black characters.

dialogue.speaker: Dave Chappelle
dialogue.line: First I'm hootie, Now I'm fucking kunte kinte, The deejay?

jokes.text: Cocksucker? I don't know. Mother fucker? I don't know.
jokes.setup: Bridget worried about swearing on TV
jokes.punchline: Immediately demonstrates by saying the exact words she's worried about
jokes.explanation: Classic ironic structure - expressing worry by doing the exact thing she's worried about. Shows nervous anxiety overriding judgment.

dialogue.speaker: Bridget Fonda
dialogue.line: Cocksucker? I don't know. Mother fucker? I don't know.

jokes.text: Is the whole family Like this?
jokes.setup: Bridget Fonda's neurotic behavior about swearing
jokes.punchline: Larry's dry observation about the famous Fonda family
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry deadpan - economical and cutting. References the famous Fonda family's reputation for activism and intensity.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Is the whole family Like this?

jokes.text: Slide it under, Asshole. Nice going, dipshit. Give me my pen back, Schmuck.
jokes.setup: Hank trying to borrow pen through bathroom door
jokes.punchline: Increasingly hostile exchange through bathroom stall
jokes.explanation: Bathroom door physical comedy with escalating insults. The mundane pen request becomes increasingly aggressive.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Slide it under, Asshole.

dialogue.speaker: Sid
dialogue.line: Nice going, dipshit.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Give me my pen back, Schmuck.

jokes.text: By the way, I already have A travel clock.
jokes.setup: Previous discussion about Larry's cheap gift
jokes.punchline: Hank's passive-aggressive way of rejecting Larry's cheapness
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank passive-aggression. Calls back to the travel clock discussion while asserting his superiority through ownership.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: By the way, I already have A travel clock.

jokes.text: He's got A very firm ass.
jokes.setup: Context suggests discussion about Hank's behavior
jokes.punchline: Unexpected physical observation about Hank
jokes.explanation: Unexpected comment creates awkward moment. The specificity and inappropriateness make it memorable but not particularly crafted.

dialogue.speaker: Bridget Fonda
dialogue.line: He's got A very firm ass.

jokes.text: [Hank getting Bridget to sign autograph 'on his lap']
jokes.setup: Standard autograph request setup
jokes.punchline: Visual of where Hank wants the autograph placed
jokes.explanation: Classic Hank inappropriate behavior. Visual comedy from the awkward positioning and Hank's obliviousness to inappropriateness.

dialogue.line: Hank getting Bridget to sign autograph 'on his lap'

jokes.text: Take your dick Out of your ear, You little troll.
jokes.setup: Artie is frustrated with Hank about the missing pen
jokes.punchline: Creates an impossible and crude anatomical insult
jokes.explanation: Quintessential Artie - creative vulgarity that's anatomically impossible but emotionally accurate

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Take your dick Out of your ear, You little troll.

jokes.text: You know What he means By his 'office?' No. The van.
jokes.setup: Hank saying he'll put autograph in his office
jokes.punchline: Reveals Hank's 'office' is actually a van
jokes.explanation: Perfect character revelation - Hank's pretensions deflated by reality. The setup and payoff structure works perfectly with Larry's dry delivery.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: You know What he means By his 'office?' No.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: The van.

jokes.text: Christ is king.
jokes.setup: Discussing Phil's recovery from illness
jokes.punchline: Artie's unexpected religious exclamation
jokes.explanation: Completely unexpected from the crude Artie character. The randomness and economy make it memorable and character-revealing.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Christ is king.

jokes.text: I swear on my mother's life That I was just sick.
jokes.setup: Phil continuing to lie about his reasons for leaving
jokes.punchline: Extreme oath for something obviously false
jokes.explanation: Shows Phil's commitment to his lie even when everyone knows the truth. The extreme oath makes his dishonesty more absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: I swear on my mother's life That I was just sick.

jokes.text: From the guy That gives out $10,000 pens? Who does that?
jokes.setup: Artie giving Larry expensive art
jokes.punchline: References Larry's supposed generosity with expensive pen
jokes.explanation: Good callback that sets up the coming revelation. Artie's amazement at Larry's supposed generosity makes the truth more impactful.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: From the guy That gives out $10,000 pens? Who does that?

jokes.text: That's a ph-1,000. That's the top Of the line. I gave you the, uh, One that cost 500.
jokes.setup: Everyone thinking Larry spent $10,000 on pen
jokes.punchline: Larry reveals he only spent $500, and Artie upgraded
jokes.explanation: Perfect character revelation - Larry's true cheapness exposed while Artie's over-reaction is revealed. Great escalation of the pen confusion.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: That's a ph-1,000. That's the top Of the line. I gave you the, uh, One that cost 500.

jokes.text: But I'm sorry you're out For $10,000?
jokes.setup: Larry realizes Artie spent $10K to replace the lost pen
jokes.punchline: His polite concern masks the absurdity of the situation
jokes.explanation: Larry's understated reaction to an completely insane financial commitment is perfect character work

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: But I'm sorry you're out For $10,000?

jokes.text: Ha ha. What the fuck?
jokes.setup: Realizing the total cost of the gift confusion
jokes.punchline: Larry's understated reaction to the $29,000 total
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry reaction - casual profanity mask his shock. The understated response to such an expensive mistake is quintessential Larry.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Ha ha. What the fuck?

jokes.text: It'll be a $29,000 story.
jokes.setup: The escalating cost of gift confusion
jokes.punchline: Larry calculating the entertainment value per dollar
jokes.explanation: Perfect episode closer - Larry's dry acceptance of the expensive mistake as entertainment value. Calls back to earlier Friar's Club reference.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: It'll be a $29,000 story.
